<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration.qmd@08816c2cb
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# MoECOT Runtime and Multi-Core Orchestration

Routing says which capability should act. Readiness gates say whether that
capability is allowed to act now. A runtime has to do the more concrete work:
accept the command, select bounded specialist lanes, apply gates, record what
happened, preserve replay handles, hand off the result, and leave residuals
where the evidence ran out.

MoECOT matters here because it gives the book a concrete runtime shape to
reason about. It is a compact-orchestrator pattern with specialist lanes,
control-plane gates, ledgers, replay, handoff, readiness updates, and explicit
limits. That makes it useful as implementation-reference context for the ASI
Stack.

The boundary is just as important as the shape. This chapter does not treat
MoECOT as reproduced evidence that the stack works. The source note, connector
context, schema, finite Lean gates, and synthetic readiness mapping help define
what runtime evidence would have to look like. They do not prove benchmark
performance, deployment readiness, model quality, specialist adequacy, security
behavior, replay correctness, or production safety.

## Problem

An architecture can sound coherent while still having no believable runtime
surface. It can name planning, routing, memory, verification, execution,
benchmarks, and self-improvement, but if there is no record that shows how a
command moved through those layers, the system is still mostly prose.

The runtime layer is where the stack has to stop being atmospheric. A command
must enter with an authority envelope. A route head must choose a lane. A
specialist core must do bounded work. A control plane must accept, deny, or
pause. Ledgers must record the decision. Replay references must exist or be
marked missing. Handoff must name the next owner. Residuals must stay visible.

MoECOT is the current reference shape for that runtime boundary. Its role is to
say what an evidence-rich multi-core orchestration packet should contain, not
to ask readers to trust a runtime name.

## Why Ordinary Runtime Stories Fail

Many runtime descriptions collapse three different things:

- an architectural intention;
- a source-reported implementation story;
- a reproduced result.

The ASI Stack cannot allow those to become one confidence label. A named
runtime can be useful and still unverified. A source can describe a benchmark
artifact without the book having reproduced the benchmark. A schema can validate
record shape without proving that a real run happened. A Lean theorem can prove
a finite gate over declared fields without proving that the underlying runtime
is safe or effective.

That distinction is the point of this chapter. MoECOT is not used as a shortcut
around evidence discipline. It is used as a stress test for it. If a compact
orchestrator, route head, specialist core, ledger, replay handle, or readiness
gate cannot say whether it is source-reported, locally reproduced, externally
corroborated, or blocked, then it is not yet usable as evidence.

The relevant external comparison is therefore orchestration and routing, not a
MoECOT benchmark claim. Planning systems, sparse expert routing, conditional
computation, and cost-quality routers help place the chapter. They do not prove
that MoECOT has matched those baselines inside this repository.

## Core Claim

MoECOT should be treated as the implementation-reference layer for a governed
low-parameter multi-core ASI stack once its authenticated source is fully mined
(evidence boundary: architectural argument).

That claim remains at `argument` support. The current evidence supports a
runtime crosswalk: compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, fail-closed control
plane, run and task ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, promotion
blockers, and residual tracking. It does not support a runtime success claim.

The fold decision also remains open. A recorded fold disposition proposes that
this chapter may later become a named MoECOT Runtime Crosswalk section inside
`routing-heads-and-specialist-cores`. That fold has not executed. The current
reader manuscript keeps the standalone chapter for this release cycle while
preserving the future fold or restoration condition.

## Mechanism

The clean way to read MoECOT is as an evidence-packet factory.

A command arrives. The compact orchestrator receives it. The route head selects
one or more specialist lanes. Each lane receives bounded authority, bounded
context, and bounded tools. The control plane applies readiness and promotion
gates. Ledgers record the route, gate decisions, authority state, denied paths,
failed gates, replay references, handoff state, and residuals. Only then can a
future reviewer decide whether the run is a design example, source-reported
artifact, imported artifact, locally reproduced result, or blocked claim.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Command["Command with authority envelope"] --> Orchestrator["Compact orchestrator"]
  Orchestrator --> Head["Route head"]
  Head --> CoreA["Specialist core A"]
  Head --> CoreB["Specialist core B"]
  Head --> CoreC["Specialist core C"]
  CoreA --> Gates["Control-plane gates"]
  CoreB --> Gates
  CoreC --> Gates
  Gates --> Ledger["Run, task, and authority ledgers"]
  Ledger --> Replay["Replay refs and missing-replay markers"]
  Replay --> Decision{"Promotion allowed?"}
  Decision -- "yes, within scope" --> Handoff["Handoff or delivery"]
  Decision -- "no" --> Residual["Residual escrow and blockers"]
  Handoff --> Readiness["Readiness update"]
  Residual --> Readiness
```

The diagram is not a deployed runtime claim. It is the record path a runtime
would have to satisfy before stronger claims were allowed.

The important design move is that denied paths are first-class. A missing replay
reference, failed gate, denied route, absent ledger, unresolved handoff, or
source-only field is not a footnote. It is runtime state. It explains why a
claim cannot promote.

## The Orchestration Record

The minimum public boundary is the `MoECOT Orchestration Record`. It should
make the runtime answer ordinary review questions without appealing to the
runtime's name.

The record needs a run identity, command reference, orchestrator identity,
route head, specialist cores, control-plane gates, route-authority ledger,
ordinary ledger references, readiness-gate references, replay references,
denied routes, failed gates, missing replay references, handoff references,
promotion blockers, residuals, source-reported fields, locally reproduced
fields, externally corroborated fields, blocked fields, source-claim state, and
non-claims.

Those fields keep useful but weak evidence from laundering itself. A packet can
be complete as a design example and still empty as a reproduced benchmark. It
can be source-reported but not imported. It can have a schema-valid shape while
still lacking the replay references that would justify promotion. It can be
valuable for prototype planning while remaining unusable as support for a
chapter-core claim.

## Invariants

The runtime invariant is traceability across layers. A runtime route should be
traceable to a command, route choice, specialist lane, authority envelope,
gate decision, ledger entry, replay state, handoff owner, and residual set.
Without that trace, orchestration is bypassing the stack rather than
implementing it.

The evidence invariant is separation. Source-reported fields, locally
reproduced fields, externally corroborated fields, and blocked fields must not
collapse into a single "implemented" label.

The promotion invariant is fail-closed. Runtime promotion requires readiness,
regression, and replay evidence references. A runtime claim sourced only from
unavailable or uninspected text cannot move above argument support. The Lean
module for this chapter records those gates as finite predicates. That is
useful traceability, not proof of runtime behavior.

## Failure Modes

The first failure mode is implementation branding as proof. A runtime can have
a good name, a plausible diagram, and persuasive source language while still
failing to produce public evidence packets.

The second failure mode is replay theater. A runtime preserves a replay handle
for the success path while failed gates, denied routes, human interventions,
missing sources, and residual branches disappear. That kind of replay can make
an orchestrator look cleaner than it is.

The third failure mode is authority drift. Specialist lanes can become useful
enough that the system starts granting them broader context, broader tools, or
replacement status without recording the authority delta. Multi-core
orchestration without an authority ledger becomes parallelized permission
creep.

The fourth failure mode is evidence-state collapse. A source-reported benchmark
artifact becomes a book-verified benchmark. A schema-valid fixture becomes a
runtime replay. A finite Lean gate becomes a safety proof. The chapter exists
to keep those collapses visible.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable MoECOT artifact is not a full runtime. It is a public-safe
orchestration packet that proves the book knows what to ask for.

The smallest useful packet would include one accepted command, one denied route,
one failed or missing replay reference, one residual handoff, one promotion
blocker, and the explicit split between source-reported, locally reproduced,
externally corroborated, and blocked fields. That packet could validate the
record shape without claiming deployment.

Current implemented support is narrower than that. The repository has the
schema for the orchestration record, finite Lean gates over declared promotion
fields, and a synthetic readiness/residual mapping slice. The MoECOT experiment
directory remains planned. No MoECOT runtime artifact, replay record, benchmark
record, current report bundle, worker/core balance trace, isolation result, or
local run has been imported or reproduced.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is not a larger agent loop. It is a runtime that produces
reviewable evidence as a normal side effect of doing work.

In that end state, a compact orchestrator would receive typed commands, lease
specialist lanes, enforce authority and context boundaries, run or deny work,
record gate decisions, emit replay references, hand off artifacts, preserve
failed branches, and update readiness without smuggling trust through branding.
The product would be a chain of evidence packets: command packet, route packet,
specialist packet, control-plane packet, replay packet, handoff packet, and
residual packet.

That is beyond the current repository. The current chapter can describe the
target and validate some record discipline. It cannot claim that MoECOT already
achieves the target.

## Summary

MoECOT gives the book a concrete runtime shape, and the book gives MoECOT a
strict evidence boundary. The result is a useful implementation-reference lane:
specific enough to guide future prototypes, but bounded enough that runtime
specificity does not become proof.

The open work is direct and practical. Import or generate public-safe runtime
packets. Preserve denied paths and missing evidence. Replay what can be replayed
and mark what cannot. Keep source-reported fields separate from locally
reproduced fields. Only then can MoECOT move from runtime crosswalk to stronger
implementation evidence.

## Handoff

The next question is where such a runtime lands. A route can choose a specialist
and a runtime can produce an evidence packet, but actual work still happens on
devices, nodes, stores, runners, and rented machines with identity, privacy,
cost, locality, energy, and revocation constraints. Personal Compute Hives and
Federated Edge Intelligence moves from orchestration records to the governed
compute substrate underneath them.
